author: @Alkimi
text:
100% Great Wisdom, as always. 
As far as the electric toothbrush goes, though...😂 I was convinced to buy one by a colleague who said "if you get the ultra-sonic high-speed one, the top of the line, that one vibrates so fast that it rattles loose gunk from deep within the crevices, cleaning more completely than is possible by any other method" 
It seemed plausible to me, so I got that one. 😂 I hardly use it 😂 it's a lot of bother and the sensation is quite intense and uncomfortable. I've always been bad about dental care 😬

author: @bahmanaghdaie9496
text: Awesome life advice. Thank you.

author: @sailingoctopus1
text:
You've mentioned your opinion of electric toothbrushes before and I commented then that you are wrong, so I guess I've got to tell you again that you are wrong.

You could clean your teeth with an ordinary toothbrush for two hours longer and you still wouldn't get them as clean as if you use an ultrasonic electric toothbrush.

Surely you must have used an ultrasonic parts cleaner, which cleans parts better than anything else?

People that clean their teeth with an ultrasonic electric toothbrush have much healthier teeth and gums. Cleaning their teeth using a conventional toothbrush just doesn't have the same effect.

OK, so it's not essential to have healthy teeth and gums, but it's pretty high up on the priority list of most people.

There are plenty of better examples of things people buy that they don't need, such as juice extractors, slow cookers and breadmakers, so it seems strange that you should pick on something that genuinely benefits people's health.

author: @dennisolsson3119
text:
Teeth are kind of nasty to loose. In sweden the guideline is 2 minutes of brushing, 2 times per day.

And as others have said: it is not something I need, but it does a eay better job than I would in the same time. I also use a washing machine instead of washing the clothes by hand or not at all.

author: @wrekced
text: Your take on electric toothbrushes is totally bunk because you are ignoring disabled people who may have difficulty brushing their teeth. Just because YOU do not need something does not mean that nobody needs it. With my multiple illnesses, brushing my teeth can be exhasting! Your position has all the nuance and subtlety of a jakhammer! Try thinking about the limitations of others before you declare that nobody needs X thing. My electric toothbrush allows me to brush my teeth without having to take breaks and rest.

replies.text: really? Can you not see a metaphor when it is biting you in the ass? This as nothing to do with the electric toothbrush - I am using that as a metaphor for a wasteful society - I am amazed I have to explain that!!

author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
text: I have an electric fly swat the shape of a tennis racket. you hold the button in and as the fly or mosquitoe flies into the racket grid, there is a cracking pop and a flash of lightning. It's so satisfyin and actually makes me enjoy having mosquitoes. As I live in South Africa, it's one main reason I wanted to move to UK, but my useful BA can't get me to enter the country! I mean really two reason for me wanting to emigrate to UK: flies an mosquitoes. But, there's much to be said for the eletric tennis-racket zapper!

The Slippery slope of a post-date society.

author: @ColinMcNulty
text: Electric toothbrushes: try one, you might like it. They clean your teeth far better than you can do yourself.

author: @snowwhitehair485
text: The electric toothbrush has always been a moot point for me. In around 1966/7, (when I was around age 13/14), the BBC children's pseudo-science TV programme 'Tom-Tom' ran an inventor's competition.  Being a science nerd I had the bright idea of inventing an electric toothbrush because it did not yet exist.  I entered the design idea into the competition but despite its operation being very similar to modern products it did not win, but I still have the Tom-Tom badge that all competition entrants were sent as a consolation prize.  Within 2 years 'Philips' had brought out the world's first commercial electric toothbrush.  I still sometimes wonder when exactly they came up with that idea. Clearly I was not the one to be destined for greatness by inventing this ubiquitous product.  I told this story to my dental hygienist recently but I don't think she believed me.

author: @Jarvis30
text:
Playing Devils Advocate here.... why do you care about others having electric toothbrushes.  
That the European socialist control mechanism showing itself.  
Electric toothbrushes are what make people different. Why have a drone or a 3D printer?   Its not about the item at all, its about what makes people happy it their lives.  My drone is fun, my 3D printer is helpful, and i love my smartphone, but i could survive without them.
The whole concept of capitalism is if people didn't like electric toothbrushes they wouldn't buy them and the electric toothbrush company would go out of business.  
The problem is that just the conversation Rob brings up will trigger some nut to glue their body to the doors of an electric toothbrush company and create some kind of movement to ban them. 🤔

author: @richardclark5112
text: To me, the main benefit of an electric oscillating toothbrush is that is can get right to the back of your teeth and have effect, where a standard brush has no scrubbing ability at its point of direction change, an electric can sit there and go at it all day.

author: @frankfaubert1927
text:
Your dentist might be leading you down the path a bit. 

It's a minimum of 30 seconds per surface so, a minimum of 90 seconds or a minute and a half of tooth brushing. Most electric toothbrushes have a 2 minute timer divided into 4-30 second intervals, 30 seconds for each surface and 30 seconds for an overall cleaning.

No, I'm not a dentist but i did work as a digital technician in the dental industry for over 20 years. You pick things up, lol.

If my comment isn't the epitome of UFI, I don't know what is, lol.

author: @orpheuscreativeco9236
text:
I was considering using an electric toothbrush in a resonator chamber to excite water molecules in the hopes that it would aid in HHO production. 🤔 I don't know that it would work, but the concept is a matching the resonance to help break apart the molecular bonds 🤷 Who knows!?

I bought an electric toothbrush after a painful trip to the dentist a couple of years ago, and I've still got it wrapped up in the packaging in the cupboard 😂

replies.author: @elvendragonhammer5433
replies.text:
I have 3, one for my teeth- (I still have my wisdom teeth that didn't need to be removed but were a nightmage to clean where they meet my gums in back <there's only a few millimeters of space between my tendon from my jaw & the teeth. Because it can face downward & occilate from that spot, the bristles can get behind it where the head of a regular brush is much too thick & cannot. It works so much better than a standard brush, or floss or mouthwash at getting rid of the buildup.) The other two ( one regular & one I took apart & melted the plastic & bent the rod to about 90 degrees) are for cleaning KVS (Kitchen Video System) & other sensitive resturaunt electronics & the case where the motherboard is usually rivited or welded into the case itself, making cleaning the grease, aerosolized oil, & food that get into them nearly impossible to clean otherwise, partly because most degreasing solvents would damage the board or HDD, NVME connectors etc. or the very little physical space & sheer # of components packed inside them. 
As forr HHO- An electric toothbrush is only going to excite the water itself, which the electricity (especially if your already using a more conductive electrolyte) will already do a better job without it. You'd need something like an ultrasonic humidifier or ultrasonic cleaner (cleaner is better) instead, (it will need to be both electricly & chemicly isolated) but any other chemical, even table salt is going to change the resonant frequency that the molocules split at, because you're no longer trying to split water, but rather a compound. You'd likely need some other hardware & software so you can probe like with an oscilliscope & adjust the frequncy of the piezo or ultrasonic transducer itself. You'd also either have to make a perfect chemical compound that you can repeat down to it's molar weight etc (every time you need to change the electrollyte) & you'd still need go through this tuning process multiple times during the same run because as metal ions start transferring into the water that harmonic frequency is going to continue to shift.

Isolation of the piezo would be difficult because all good HHO electrolytes are very chemicly active & can damage some rubber, silicone, most metals, soft or high oil content plastics & even things like 2-part epoxies. In addition some motherboards/IC's that control the piezo require a 3rd wire (sometimes a probe, sometimes it electrolizes the water itself) those wouldn't work & will short because of the current in the electrolyte & I've only seen one reletively cheap option that didn't need one- some random Owl "cold air" humidifier. (though that one still had a magnetic reed switch I had to deal with.)

You should also take note if you are using low grade metals, even steel in an HHO gen you could contaminaate the electrolite with things like hexaflorane which will be poisonous (for you to breathe or touch) as well as acidic & is illegal to dispose of in both the enviornment & water except @ designated facilities (especially in a toilet/sink) where you will be exposing others to it once it gets back into the public water supply. A standard water treatment facility doesn't have the equipment or chemicals on hand to remove something like that. All of the major cities have sensors in pipe junctions to find out where stuff like that is coming from. And the treatment plant (even without any sensors) will have to document the discovery of it in their monthly water report. (which will likey be investigated by the EPA shortly after, assuming your in the US.)
My suggestion is use either good stainless steel or graphite electodes instead to alleviate the poisoning issue. (you still need to dispose of the electrolite properly anyway even w/o the added contamination).

replies.author: @orpheuscreativeco9236
replies.text:
@elvendragonhammer5433 Thanks for the tips! I was leaning towards ultrasonic frequency initially (though I do want to avoid heating the water rapidly), so I appreciate the reminder 👍  I was thinking about a chamber that had more bell like properties, like an enameled cast iron pot or the like. I assume the enamel would act as an isolating barrier between the iron and the water w/electrolyte. I'm no chemist so I will need to do thorough research first 👍 I would prefer to avoid creating toxic byproducts from the conversation. 

Thanks again for the insight ✌️

Also have noticed I don't injure my gums near as often due to my overzealous clumsiness and injured shoulder.

author: @Vibe77Guy
text: I designed a robotic assembly machine to manufacture the disposable electric toothbrush tips, for the American patent holder of the rotating tip electric toothbrush. That patent is why most other manufacturers are forced to use oscillating brushes instead of rotating tips.
